9 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

1. What is JavaScript?
JavaScript is a programming language originally created in 1995 by Brendan Eich in just 10 days while he was working at Netscape. It was built to add interactivity to web pages, enabling features like form validation, animations, and pop-ups.
Today, JavaScript is everywhere:
In your browser: Every interactive website uses it, including Facebook, YouTube, and Gmail.
On servers: You can build backend APIs through Node.js.
In mobile apps: You can build applications using frameworks like React Native.
In desktop apps: Applications like VS Code are built using JavaScript via Electron.
Beyond the screen: It is used in smart devices, games, and even robots.
The “engine” that runs JavaScript in your browser is a dedicated piece of software. Chrome uses V8, Firefox uses SpiderMonkey, and Safari uses JavaScriptCore. Node.js also uses the V8 engine, which is exactly how JavaScript is able to run outside of a web browser.

2. Your First Program
console.log() prints something to the console and is the most-used tool in a JavaScript developer’s life.
console.log("Hello,World!");
console.log(789);
console.log(true);
console.log("My name is", "Abhay", "and I am", 25, "years old");You can pass multiple values separated by commas, and console.log will print them with spaces in between.
Other useful console methods:
console.log("Normal message");
console.warn("This is a warning"); // Shown in yellow in browsers
console.error("This is an error"); // Shown in red
console.table([1, 2, 3]); // Prints data as a table
3. Comments
Comments are notes left for humans;
JavaScript completely ignores them.
// This is a single-line comment
/*This is amulti-line comment*/
console.log("Hello"); // You can also comment at the end of a lineWhy write comments?
To remind yourself what the code does.
To temporarily disable code while testing.
To explain why something is done a certain way.

4. Variables — The Heart of Programming
A variable is a named box where you store a value. Later, you can use the name to retrieve the value or change it. JavaScript has three keywords to declare variables: var, let, and const.
var age = 25;
let name = "Aman";
const PI = 3.14159;Declaration vs. Initialization
Declaration: Creating the variable. (let x;)
Initialization: Giving it a value. (x = 10;)
Combined: You can do both at once. (let x = 10;)
let city; // Declared, value is undefined
console.log(city); // undefined
city = "Bhopal"; // Initialized now
console.log(city); // BhopalBasic Differences Between var, let, and const
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Best Practices:
Use const by default.
Use let if you know the value will change.
Avoid var in modern code due to its scoping quirks.
const country = "India";
let score = 0;
score = score + 10; // Works perfectly
// country = "USA"; // Error: Assignment to constant variableNaming Rules
Must start with a letter, _, or $.
Can contain letters, digits, _, or $.
Cannot start with a digit.
Cannot use reserved keywords (like let, if, function).
Case-sensitive: age and Age are treated as different variables.
Naming Conventions (Good Style)
Use camelCase for variables: firstName, totalAmount, userAge.
Use UPPER_SNAKE_CASE for true constants: MAX_USERS, PI.
Use meaningful names: let a = 5 is bad practice, while let studentCount = 5 is good.

5. Data Types
Every value in JavaScript has a specific type, broken down into two main categories.
Primitive Types (7)
Type Example
string ”Hello”, ‘JS’, `template`
number 42, 3.14, -7
boolean true, false
null null (intentional empty value)
undefined undefined (no value assigned yet)
symbol Symbol(“id”) (advanced, rarely used early on)
bigint 9007199254740993n (for exceptionally huge numbers)
Non-Primitive Type
object: This covers standard objects, arrays, functions, and dates.
let name = "Aman"; // string
let age = 25; // number
let isStudent = true; // boolean
let car = null; // null ("no car right now, intentionally")
let job; // undefined (never assigned)
let id = Symbol("uid"); // symbol
let bigNum = 1234567890n; // bigint (note the 'n')A Common Confusion: null vs undefined
undefined means: “This variable was never given a value.”
null means: “I am intentionally setting this to nothing.”
let a; // undefined (JavaScript gave it this value)
let b = null; // null (You gave it this value)
6. The typeof Operator
The typeof operator tells you the type of any given value.
console.log(typeof "hello"); // "string"
console.log(typeof 42); // "number"
console.log(typeof true); // "boolean"
console.log(typeof undefined); // "undefined"
console.log(typeof null); // "object" - famous bug in JS!
console.log(typeof {}); // "object"
console.log(typeof []); // "object" (arrays are objects)
console.log(typeof function(){});// "function"Note: typeof null returning “object” is a famous bug from 1995 that was never fixed because too much legacy code depends on it.

7. Type Conversion vs. Type Coercion
This is one of JavaScript’s most famous quirks.
Explicit Conversion (You do it on purpose)
let str = "42";
let num = Number(str); // converts "42" to 42
console.log(typeof num); // "number"let n = 100;
let s = String(n); // converts 100 to "100"
let val = "hello";
let b = Boolean(val); // true (non-empty strings are truthy)
Implicit Coercion (JavaScript does it automatically)
console.log("5" + 3); // "53" (string concatenation)
console.log("5" - 3); // 2 (number subtraction)
console.log("5" * "2"); // 10
console.log(true + 1); // 2 (true becomes 1)
console.log(false + 1); // 1 (false becomes 0)
console.log(null + 1); // 1 (null becomes 0)
console.log(undefined + 1); // NaN (undefined becomes NaN)The + operator is special — if one side is a string, it concatenates. All other math operators attempt to convert the values to numbers.
Truthy and Falsy Values
In a boolean context, every value is evaluated as either truthy or falsy.
Falsy values (memorize these 6):
false, 0, “” (empty string), null, undefined, NaN
Everything else is truthy — including “0”, “false”, [], and {}.
if ("hello") console.log("truthy"); // runs
if (0) console.log("won't run");
if ([]) console.log("truthy"); // runs! empty arrays are truthy
8. Operators
Arithmetic Operators :
let a = 10, b = 3;
console.log(a + b); // 13 (addition)
console.log(a - b); // 7 (subtraction)
console.log(a * b); // 30 (multiplication)
console.log(a / b); // 3.333... (division)
console.log(a % b); // 1 (modulus/remainder)
console.log(a ** b); // 1000 (exponentiation, 10^3)Increment and Decrement :
let x = 5;
x++; // x is now 6 (post-increment)
++x; // x is now 7 (pre-increment)
x--; // x is now 6
--x; // x is now 5Difference between pre and post:
let x = 5;
let y = x++; // y gets 5 (old value), THEN x becomes 6
let z = ++x; // x becomes 7 first, THEN z gets 7Assignment Operators:
let x = 10;
x += 5; // x = x + 5 -> 15
x -= 3; // x = x - 3 -> 12
x *= 2; // x = x * 2 -> 24
x /= 4; // x = x / 4 -> 6
x %= 4; // x = x % 4 -> 2Comparison Operators :
console.log(5 == "5"); // true (loose equality - converts types)
console.log(5 === "5"); // false (strict equality - checks type AND value)
console.log(5 != "5"); // false
console.log(5 !== "5"); // true
console.log(5 > 3); // true
console.log(5 <= 5); // trueGolden Rule: Always use === and !==. Avoid == and != because they trigger type coercion and cause hidden bugs.
console.log(0 == false); // true - surprising!
console.log("" == false); // true - surprising!
console.log(null == undefined); // true - surprising!
console.log(0 === false); // false - saneLogical Operators :
let a = true, b = false;
console.log(a && b); // false (AND: both must be true)
console.log(a || b); // true (OR: at least one must be true)
console.log(!a); // false (NOT: flips the value)Short-circuit behavior:
&& returns the first falsy value, or the last value if all are truthy.
|| returns the first truthy value, or the last value if all are falsy.
console.log("hello" && "world"); // "world"
console.log(0 && "hello"); // 0
console.log("" || "default"); // "default"
console.log("user" || "guest"); // "user"This is highly useful for setting default values:
let username = userInput || "Guest";Ternary Operator (Shorthand if-else)
Syntax: condition ? valueIfTrue : valueIfFalse
let age = 20;
let status = age >= 18 ? "Adult" : "Minor";
console.log(status); // "Adult"
9. Strings
A string is a sequence of characters wrapped in quotes.
let s1 = 'Single quotes';
let s2 = "Double quotes";
let s3 = `Backticks (template literals)`;String Concatenation vs. Template Literals
// Concatenation
let firstName = "Aman";
let lastName = "Kumar";
let fullName = firstName + " " + lastName;// Template Literals (Modern and Preferred)
let name = "Aman";
let age = 25;
console.log(`Hello, my name is ${name} and I am ${age} years old.`);
Template literals also support multi-line strings naturally:
let poem = `Roses are red,
Violets are blue,
JS is awesome,
And so are you.`;let s = "Hello, World!";
console.log(s.length); // 13
console.log(s.toUpperCase()); // "HELLO, WORLD!"
console.log(s.toLowerCase()); // "hello, world!"
console.log(s.indexOf("World")); // 7 (position of "World")
console.log(s.includes("Hello")); // true
console.log(s.slice(0, 5)); // "Hello"
console.log(s.substring(7, 12)); // "World"
console.log(s.replace("World", "JS")); // "Hello, JS!"
console.log(s.split(", ")); // ["Hello", "World!"]
console.log(" hi ".trim()); // "hi"
console.log("abc".repeat(3)); // "abcabcabc"
console.log(s.startsWith("Hello")); // true
console.log(s.endsWith("!")); // true
console.log(s.charAt(0)); // "H"
console.log(s[0]); // "H" (also works)Important: Strings are immutable. Methods don’t change the original string; they return an entirely new string.
let x = "hello";
x.toUpperCase();
console.log(x); // "hello" remains unchanged!x = x.toUpperCase();
console.log(x); // "HELLO"

10. Numbers
JavaScript has only one number type — both integers and decimals are categorized simply as number.
let int = 42;
let float = 3.14;
let negative = -100;
let exponent = 5e3; // 5000Useful Number Methods
let n = 3.14159;
console.log(n.toFixed(2)); // "3.14" (returns a string!)
console.log(Number("42")); // 42
console.log(Number("42abc")); // NaN
console.log(parseInt("42px")); // 42 (parses what it can)
console.log(parseFloat("3.14kg")); // 3.14
console.log(isNaN("hello")); // true
console.log(Number.isInteger(5)); // true
console.log(Number.isInteger(5.5)); // falseThe Math Object
Math is a built-in object providing mathematical methods and constants.
console.log(Math.PI); // 3.14159...
console.log(Math.E); // 2.71828...
console.log(Math.round(4.6)); // 5
console.log(Math.floor(4.9)); // 4 (always rounds down)
console.log(Math.ceil(4.1)); // 5 (always rounds up)
console.log(Math.abs(-7)); // 7
console.log(Math.max(1, 5, 3)); // 5
console.log(Math.min(1, 5, 3)); // 1
console.log(Math.pow(2, 10)); // 1024
console.log(Math.sqrt(16)); // 4
console.log(Math.random()); // random number between 0 and 1Common pattern to generate a random integer between min and max:
let rand = Math.floor(Math.random() * (max - min + 1)) + min;
11. Conditionals
Conditionals allow your program to make decisions.
if / else if / else
let marks = 75;if (marks >= 90) {
console.log("A grade");
} else if (marks >= 75) {
console.log("B grade");
} else if (marks >= 50) {
console.log("C grade");
} else {
console.log("Fail");
}
Conditions are evaluated from top to bottom. The first matching block executes, and the rest are skipped.
Nested if
let age = 20;
let hasLicense = true;if (age >= 18) {
if (hasLicense) {
console.log("Can drive");
} else {
console.log("Get a license first");
}
} else {
console.log("Too young to drive");
}
The switch Statement
When you have multiple possible values for a single variable, switch is often cleaner than a long chain of if-else blocks.
let day = "Monday";switch (day) {
case "Monday":
console.log("Start of the week");
break;
case "Friday":
console.log(
